coredata.dc:title: Graphdiyne nanosheet as a novel sensing platform for self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence of MOF enriched ruthenium (II) in the presence of dual co-reactants for detection of tumor marker
coredata.dc:creator: Bahari, Delnia
Babamiri, Bahareh
Moradi, Kayvan
Salimi, Abdollah
Hallaj, Rahman
coredata.dc:description: Graphdiyne (GDY) is a new two-dimensional carbon material with high charge carrier mobility, excellent conductivity, more suitable band gap, and natural pores was introduced as a new electrochemiluminescent sensing platform. Herein, the metal organic framework (MOFs) used for enrichment of luminophore with grafting Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+(Ru-complex) and Ru-complex amine-rich nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots(Ru-NCNDs) via both encapsulating and external decoration and decoration of SmS2 QDs as coreactant. Then, the MOF enriched Ru-complex (Ru@MOF@NCNDs-Ru@SmS2 QD) located on a GDY modified ITO electrode developed as a novel and efficient ECL platform. According to the Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculation, the band gap of graphdiyne/Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ system decreased compared to graphdiyne, Ru-complex and also graphene oxide/Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+system, which enhanced (2 folds) the signal response of the presented ECL platform. The ECL response signal of the suggested emitter with high ECL efficiency (13.34%) increased 8 and 4 folds compared to GDY/Ru-NCNDs and GDY/Ru@MOF@NCNDs-Ru as platforms, respectively. The proposed ECL platform applied for CA19-9 antigens detection at concentration range 0.0005 UmL−1 to 200 UmL−1 and detection limit of 0.00013 UmL−1.The development of GDY based platform for decorating nano luminophores, not only provides the design of ECL luminophores with high performance but also promises the application of the presented strategy for fabrication of ultrasensitive bio affinity sensors as candidates in clinical monitoring and diseases diagnostics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphdyine
Metal organic framework
Self enhanced electrochemiluminesence
Ru-complex
Density functional theory
Immunosensor
CA19-9 cancer marker

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon nanodots (NCNDs) and SmS2 QDs
Synthesis of [Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)] (PF6)2 and preparation of the Ru(bpy)2(phen–NH2)–NCNDs
Characterization of GDY and its effects on the ECL response of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) 2+
Comparison of the ECL responses with different signal probes
The ECL efficiency of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)@MOF@NCND- Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) platform
Selectivity, stability and reproducibility of the ECL immunosensor
Analytical performance of the ECL immunosensor in CA19-9 detection
Analytical application of the immunosensor in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research Office of the University of Kurdistan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative and the Research Office of the University of Kurdistan (grant number 4.1404086 ). We thanks Noor Clinical Laboratory to accommodate serum samples and ELISA analysis.

This research was supported by the Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative and the Research Office of the University of Kurdistan (grant number 4.1404086). We thanks Noor Clinical Laboratory to accommodate serum samples and ELISA analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of NCNDs, SEM images of Ru@MOF (B), Ru@ NCND- Ru (C) and the enlarged SEM image of Ru@MOF@ NCND- Ru (D) for indicating the present of Ru-NCND on the surface of Ru@MOF, (E) FT-IR spectra of NCND (green), Ru@MOF (red) and Ru@MOF@NCND (blue), (F) TEM image of SmS2QD, (G) SEM image of GDY, (H) TEM image of GDY, (I) Full-spectrum X-Ray scan and (J) high-resolution C 1s XPS peak of GDY respectively. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The HOMO and LUMO energy levels and corresponding electron distributions based on the optimization of the ground state labels 1–5 obtained using DFT calculations.

(A) ECL emission of Ru@MOF@NCND- Ru@SmS2 on the Naﬁon/ITO (a), GO/Naﬁon/ITO (b) and GDY/Naﬁon/ITO (c), The concentration of Ru@MOF@NCND-Ru@SmS2 was 2 mg mL−1. (B) ECL response of Ru/NCNDs (a) and Ru-NCNDs systems (b), ECL profiles of the different modiﬁed ITO: (C) Ru-NCNDs/Naﬁon/ITO (a), Ru@MOF@NCND/Naﬁon/ITO (b) and MOF@NCND-Ru/Naﬁon/ITO (c), (D) Ru-NCNDs (a), Ru@MOF@NCND- Ru/Naﬁon/ITO (b) and (b)+loaded SmS2(c). The concentration of all probes was 5 mg mL−1.

(A) EIS of (a) ITO/nafion/graphdiyne; (b): ITO/nafion/graphdiyne/Ru@MOF@NCNDs- Ru@SmS2QD; (c): ITO/nafion/graphdiyne/Ru@MOF@NCNDs-Ru@SmS2QD + Ab1; (d): ITO/nafion/graphdiyne/Ru@MOF@NCNDs-Ru@SmS2QD + Ab1+ BSA; (e): ITO/nafion/graphdiyne/Ru@MOF@NCNDs- Ru@SmS2QD + Ab1+ BSA + AgCA19-9 in a solution of 0.1 M KCl containing 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]2−/[Fe(CN)6]3−, applied potential was 0.25 V at the frequency range of 0.1 Hz–10 kHz. (B) The ECL intensities response of the immunosensor in the presence of CA19-9, CA15-3, CA125, AFP, CEA, HBsAg with the same concentration (50 U mL−1) and the mixed samples. (C) ECL stability of the ECL immunosensor for 10 consequence detection in the presence of 15U mL−1 CA19-9 antigen. (D) The ECL intensities responses of the immunosensor for CA19-9 antigen with different concentrations (a–n): 0.0005, 0.001, 0.006, 0.02, 0.08, 0.15, 0.5, 2, 15, 30, 50, 100, 200 U mL−1 in 0.1 M pH 7.4 PBS, scanning from 0 V to 1.5 V with a scan rate of 100 mV s−1, Inset: Linear plot of the ECL intensity vs logarithm of CA19-9 concentration.

Schematic diagram for the construction of ECL immunosensor. (A) Preparation process of Ru@MOF@NCNDs-Ru signal probe. (B) Scheme of the ECL immunosensor fabrication for CA19-9 detection. (C) The possible ECL mechanism of Ru@MOF@NCND-Ru@SmS2QD/GDY-ITO system.
A recovery study carried out in serum samples for CA19-9 Ag detection by the proposed immunosensor.

head.ce:title: Graphdiyne nanosheet as a novel sensing platform for self-enhanced electrochemiluminescence of MOF enriched ruthenium (II) in the presence of dual co-reactants for detection of tumor marker

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Center for Nanotechnology, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, University of Kurdistan, 66177-15175, Sanandaj, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphdiyne (GDY) is a new two-dimensional carbon material with high charge carrier mobility, excellent conductivity, more suitable band gap, and natural pores was introduced as a new electrochemiluminescent sensing platform. Herein, the metal organic framework (MOFs) used for enrichment of luminophore with grafting Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+(Ru-complex) and Ru-complex amine-rich nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots(Ru-NCNDs) via both encapsulating and external decoration and decoration of SmS2 QDs as coreactant. Then, the MOF enriched Ru-complex (Ru@MOF@NCNDs-Ru@SmS2 QD) located on a GDY modified ITO electrode developed as a novel and efficient ECL platform. According to the Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculation, the band gap of graphdiyne/Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ system decreased compared to graphdiyne, Ru-complex and also graphene oxide/Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+system, which enhanced (2 folds) the signal response of the presented ECL platform. The ECL response signal of the suggested emitter with high ECL efficiency (13.34%) increased 8 and 4 folds compared to GDY/Ru-NCNDs and GDY/Ru@MOF@NCNDs-Ru as platforms, respectively. The proposed ECL platform applied for CA19-9 antigens detection at concentration range 0.0005 UmL−1 to 200 UmL−1 and detection limit of 0.00013 UmL−1.The development of GDY based platform for decorating nano luminophores, not only provides the design of ECL luminophores with high performance but also promises the application of the presented strategy for fabrication of ultrasensitive bio affinity sensors as candidates in clinical monitoring and diseases diagnostics.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphdiyne (GDY), with high degrees of π-conjugated carbon material, is a ﬂat material with sp2-and sp hybridized carbon atoms, which has attracted great consideration from fundamental studies and practical applications because of new physical and chemical properties (Li et al., 2019; Yu et al., 2019). Two-dimensional layers of GDY composed of benzene rings and acetylene units, due to the more suitable band gap for semiconductors relative to graphene, high electrical conductivity, better dispersion in aqueous solution, uniformly distributed pore structure and high chemical, electrochemical and thermal stability (Wu et al., 2020), has been expressed extensively performance in promising applications, such as catalysis (Zhang et al., 2015), lithium-ion battery storage (Huang et al., 2015), electrocatalytic oxygen reduction (Zhao et al., 2018), sensors (Zhuang et al., 2017), antimicrobial (Gu et al., 2017), biological protection and cancer therapy (Jin et al., 2018). Furthermore, theoretical studies using the Density-functional theory (DFT) confirms the suitability of GDY for catalytic process, electrode materials preparation and semiconductor devices fabrication (Wang et al., 2018). In addition, GDY can be used as efficient platform for development of sensitive sensors and biosensors. Between different sensing systems such as electrochemical immunoassay (Yang et al., 2015), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (Parker et al., 1992), radioimmunoassay (Song et al., 2015), fluorescence assay (Alarfaj et al., 2018) and electrogenerated chemiluminescence (ECL), the ECL has the privileged merit of both electrochemical and chemiluminescent assay such as high sensitivity and speciﬁcity, low background signal, simple instrumentation, extended dynamic range, and low cost (Babamiri et al., 2018a, 2018b, 2019).

As one of the most extensively studied ECL luminophore, Ru(bpy)3 2+ possesses distinct advantages such as high quantum yield and ECL efficiency, good photochemical, and chemical stability (Babamiri et al., 2017). The Ru(bpy)3 2+/tripropylamine (TPrA) system is one of the most common ECL systems in commercial ECL immunoassays because of high ECL efficiency, good biocompatibility and good water solubility (Zhang et al., 2019). However, TPrA itself has a lot of disadvantages such as toxicity, corrosive, flammability, which led to efforts to exploit alternative co-reactants (Li et al., 2015). Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots because of matching redox potentials with Ru(bpy)3 2+, high chemical stability, good solubility, swift charge transfer kinetics and rapid degradation routes, can be utilized as an appropriate alternative to the TPrA (Carrara et al., 2017). Also, NCND can covalently bonded to metal-organic complex to achieve the self-enhanced ECL for increasing the anodic ECL response owing to the shorter electron transfer route and less energy loss (Bahari et al., 2020). On the other hand, the SmS2 QDs can facilitated the generation of Ru(bpy)3 2+ radicals and generate the excited state of Ru(bpy)3 2+*, then leading to generate strong emission and it can be used as new and promising co-reactant of Ru(bpy)3 2+ (Xing et al., 2018). Furthermore, one of the most general strategies for the amplification of the ECL response is the increasing of loading capacity and the immobilizing abundant of luminophores. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are porous hybrid materials with a periodic network structure composed of modular self-assembly of nodes (metal ions or clusters) and organic bridging ligands. MOFs with large surface areas and well-defined uniform nano-scale pores and channels (Cai et al., 2018), satisfactory electrochemical stabilities (Zhou et al., 2019), adjustable composition, high permanent and easy to assemble have been widely applied in energy storage, catalysis, analytical detection, and biosensing (Lv et al., 2020a). Among these applications, the development of sensing systems based on MOF has attracted great attention (Ren et al., 2018; Lv et al., 2018, 2020b).

The sensitive ECL sensors and biosensors are developed by encapsulation or loading of Ru(bpy)3 2+ or its derivatives onto MOFs (Huang et al., 2018). However, MOFs probably have non-conductive shells that hinder electron excitation of the luminophores (Carrara et al., 2017), and decreased the ECL efficiency. To overcome this constraint, various nanomaterials such as graphene oxide (GO) (Peng et al., 2018), and carbon nanotubes (CNTs) (Jabbari et al., 2016), have been used as the sensing platform to improve the electric conductivity of MOFs and ECL efficiency.

Carbohydrate antigen 19-9 (CA19-9) as a kind of glycoprotein associated with malignant tumors is an isolated Lewis cancer biomarker of MUC1 protein for pancreatic cancer, urothelial carcinomas, colorectal cancer and gastric carcinoma, especially pancreatic carcinoma (Zhang et al., 2016), so, development of sensitive and selective method for CA19-9 detection in clinical diagnostics is a challenge.

In this study, for the first time, graphdiyne (GDY) as the novel sensing platform integrated with MOF enriched Ru-complexes as a signal probe for development of sensitive self-enhanced ECL immunosensor toward CA19-9 tumor marker detection as summarized the schematic 1. Furthermore, the sensitivity of the measuring method improved by both NCNDs, which covalently attached onto Ru-complex and also SmS2 QDs loaded onto MOF as co-reactants. The performane of the proposed platform was demonstrated by both theoretical and experimental methods. Herein, MOF encapsulated by the Ru complex and decorated with Ru-NCNDs complex was applied as the carrier for loading more luminophores and SmS2 QD decorated onto MOF applied as coreactant for improving of ECL response. The Ru@MOF@NCNDs-Ru @ SmS2 QD located on the surface of Naﬁon/GDY modified ITO electrode and after the immobilization of the primary antibody (Ab1) and incubation of CA19-9 antigens, the ECL signal measured. The application of the presented assay for CA19-9 tumor marker detection in real samples was also evaluated. The proposed immunoassay could be developed for establishing other immunoassays and measuring of different cancer markers with relevant antigens and antibodies.
All chemicals and reagents reported in SI.

The applied instruments for electrochemiluminescence and electrochemical measurements, characterization of the synthesized nanomaterials and composites are introduced in supporting information file (SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen doped carbon nanodots (NCNDs) and SmS2 QDs
ce:section.ce:para: The procedures for synthesis of NCNDs and SmS2 QDs were based on previously reports and details are reported in SI..

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of [Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)] (PF6)2 and preparation of the Ru(bpy)2(phen–NH2)–NCNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The details of the method for the synthesis of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)] (PF6)2 and Ru(bpy)2(phen–NH2)–NCNDs are given in SI file.

The Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)@MOFs (Ru@MOFs) was prepared according to the previously report with some modifications (Yang et al., 2018) and details of the procedure reported in SI. Furthermore, the synthesis of Ru@MOF@NCND-Ru illustrates in Scheme 1 A and details are presented in SI.

Typically, 500 μL as-prepared SmS2 QDs solution (2 mg mL−1) was added into 1 mL dispersed solution of Ru@MOF@NCND-Ru (5 mg mL−1) and stirred for 12 h at room temperature. The obtained Ru@MOF@NCND-Ru@SmS2 QDs was kept at 4 °C for further use.
The approach was used for the synthesis of graphdiyne like nanosheets reported in SI.

The fabrication approach of the ECL immunosensor is ilustrated in scheme 1B. At first, 10 μL of nafion (10%, diluted by ethanol) and 50 μL of graphdiyne (0.5 mg mL−1 dispersed in ethanol) were respectively coated on the cleaned ITO electrode and allowed to dry under ambient temperature for obtaining graphdiyne/nafion modified ITO. Then, 50 μL Ru@MOF@NCNDs-Ru@SmS2 QDs (2 mg mL−1) were coated the modified ITO and dried in air to get the Ru@MOF@NCNDs-Ru@SmS2 QDs/graphdiyne/nafion modified ITO film. The NH2 groups of the Ru@MOF@NCNDs-Ru@ SmS2 QDs were activated in a solution including glutaraldehyde (2.5 wt %) for 40 min. In the following, the modified ITO electrode was placed in a 0.1 M PBS solution (pH = 7.4) including 0.2 mg mL−1 Ab1CA19-9 at 37 ᵒC for 30 min and washed with PBS to remove the unbound antibodies. After that, the modified electrode was incubated with a 1.0 wt% BSA solution for 40 min to block the nonspecific binding sites. Then, the modified electrode was immersed in the PBS containing various concentrations of CA 19-9 for 30 min. Finally, the electrode was thoroughly washed with PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.40). For comparison of the ECL responses of different signal probes, the ITO electrode coated with graphdiyne/nafion, modified with NCND-Ru, Ru@MOF@NCND and MOF@NCND-Ru have been used at the same experiments.

For CA19-9 measurement, the fabricated ECL based immunosensor was immersed in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.4) containing various amount of CA19-9 antigen, and the ECL resposed were conducted via an anodic electrochemiluminescence direction with a three-electrode mode by cycling the potential from 0.0 to 1.5 V at a scan rate of 100 mV s−1 and setting the voltage of the photomultiplier tube (PMT) to 750 V by placing a band-pass filter of 620 nm.
The details of all theoretical calculations reported in SI.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of the NCNDs is shown in Fig. 1 A. The diameters of the as-prepared NCNDs were in the range of 4–7 nm particles. The characterization of NCND with other spectroscopic thecniques including, UV–vis, FT-IR, XPS and PL are reported in supporting information file (SI). The FT-IR spectroscopy measurements of the Ru(bpy)2Cl2 (a), phen-NH2 (b) and Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) complex (c) are shown in Fig. S3. As illustrated for Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2), the infrared peaks at 3454 and 3388 cm−1 corresponds to NH2 group of phen-NH2 suggests the successful formation of covalent grafting between Ru(bpy)2Cl2 complex and phen-NH2 ligand. Also, the UV–Vis spectrometry was utilized to prove the prosperous conjugation of the Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) and NCNDs. The optical absorption spectra of Ru-NCND (Fig. S4), indicate characteristic features of NCND, with n - π* transitions of CO bonds at 320 nm and distinguishable Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) absorption peaks at 285 nm and 460 nm, which confirmes the successful conjugation of NCNDs to Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2). The morphologies of Ru@MOF and Ru@MOF@NCND were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and as illustrates in (Fig. 1B) the Ru@MOF has the similar-spherical structure with the diameter of about 600 ± 50 nm. Furthermore, the SEM image of the Ru@MOF@NCND-Ru showed the surface of the globular solid structures was covered with many dots (Fig. 1C). The enlarged SEM images indicated the existence of Ru-NCND complex with the average diameter of 13 nm on the surface of Ru@MOF@NCND-Ru (Fig. 1D). The FT-IR spectroscopy studies were carried out to confirm the functional groups on the surface of MOF and MOF modified Ru-NCND. As shown in Fig. 1E, the double peaks at 3422.29 and 3454.36 cm−1 in the spectrum of Ru@MOF@NCND-Ru corresponded to NH2 groups, indicated the coordination of the amino groups of Ru-NCND with the Ru@MOF.

The size distribution and the morphological features of the SmS2 QDs were characterized by taking of TEM image (Fig. 1F). As can be seen, the as-prepared SmS2 QDs are uniform spherical particles with an average size of 6–8 nm. Also, the characterization of SmS2 QDs with other spectroscopic thecniques including, UV–vis, PL and FT-IR are reported in supporting information file (SI).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GDY and its effects on the ECL response of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) 2+
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM and TEM techniques were utilized for characterization morphology and structure of as-synthesized GDY. The SEM image in Fig. 1G indicated that GDY was composed of uniform and continuous microstructure with accumulated layers and the TEM image of the prepared GDY have the lamellar structure (Fig. 1H). Furthermore, XPS measurements were done to characterize the functional groups of GDY and to further confirm the existence of the elements. In the full XPS spectrum of the as-prepared GDY two characteristic peaks at 281.3 and 528.1 eV was observed, which were corresponded to the C1s and O1s binding peak, respectively (Fig. 1I). The expanded XPS spectra of C1s composed of four sub-peaks at 284.4, 285.1, 286.4 and 287.5 eV that can be assigned to carbon in the form of CC (sp2), CC (sp), C–OH, and CO, respectively (Fig. 1J). The observed C–O and CO peaks in GDY may be due to the adsorption of oxygen on the surface of GDY and the chemical reaction of oxygen with the terminal acetylene bonds.

For further understand the effect of GDY platform on the ECL emission of Ru-complex, the density functional theory (DFT) was implemented to investigate their frontier orbitals and the molecular orbital energy levels. For the geometry optimizations, the PF6 − counter ion was eliminated to simplify the chemical structures of these complexes. As can be seen in Fig. 2 The Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ has the lowest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy level and has the largest energy gap (ΔE) between its HOMO and related lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO). Furthermore, when Ru-complex located on a GDYand GOx, LUMO-HOMO band energy gap of these platforms were very low that the HOMO energy level of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ changed from −10.52 eV to −8.71 eV and −8.83 eV, respectively, that can be explained by the π- π stacking of complex with GDYand GOx. Overall, as shown in Table S1, the LUMO−HOMO band energy gap of three labels show an order of GDY/Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) 2+<GOX/Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) 2+< Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) 2+, which led to the easy occurrence of electron transfer reactions to generate the highest ECL efficiency. Therefore, the theoretical calculations have good agreement with the experimental results, demonstrating that DFT calculation is an effective method to confirm the application of GDY as an efficient platform for stronger ECL emission. According to the theoretical calculation, GDY has a direct band gap of 2.68 eV. When the MOF enriched Ru(II) loaded on the surface of GDY, the band gap of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) 2+ decreased from 3.19 to 1.89 eV that cause to enhance of the ECL emission intensity of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)+2. So, with the loading of the as-prepared Ru@MOF@NCND-Ru@SmS2 QDs luminophore on the surface of GDY, the ΔI ECL noticeably raised to 624 a.u., indicating that GDY due to superior electrical conductivity and the reduction band gap of Ru(II) could efficiently amplify the ECL signal of the proposed probe (Fig. 3 A). For evaluation of the effectiveness of GDY on the enhancement of ECL signal, the effect of graphene oxide on ECL emission of Ru(II) is also investigated (Fig. 3A). As illustrated compared with graphene, GDY as a platform for holding of Ru- complex has excellent barrier energy, that facilitated the generation of Ru(II) based complex radicals to produce of more efficient emitter Ru(II)*-complex. So, GDY as novel platform can enhance the ECL intensity three folds compared with graphene oxide.

Previous studies have shown that the NCNDs can be utilized as a new anodic coreactant for the ECL emission of Ru(II)-complex (Carrara et al., 2017). Furthermore, for improving the ECL intensity signal, the SmS2 QD as a novel coreactant toward Ru(II)-complex based ECL system was introduced. In addition, the ECL intensity of Ru(II)-complex in the presence of dual co-reactants (NCND and SmS2 QD) was higher than those of in NCND or SmS2 QD separately. The NCND•+ and SmS2 QD•+ cation radicals were generated by charge injection during potential cycling from 0.0 to 1.5V,at the same time,Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) 2+ was oxidized to Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2 3+. According to previous reported studies (Huang et al., 2015; Yang et al., 2019) the probable ECL mechanisms are shown as follows:
(1) NCND-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+- e−→ NCND-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)3+ (electrode oxidation)
(2) NCNDH -Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+-e-→ NCNDH•+-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+(electrode oxidation)
(3) NCNDH•+-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ - H+→ NCND• -Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+ (deprotonation)
(4) SmS2 QD-e-→ SmS2QD•+ (electrode oxidation)
(5) R . +NCND-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)3+→NCND-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)2+*+R (electron transfer)
(6) NCND-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) 2+*→ NCND-Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) 2+ + hν (luminescence)
(R represented dual coreactant NCND and SmS2 QD).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the ECL responses with different signal probes
ce:section.ce:para: Recently, in situ generating coreactant or synthesizing self-enhanced Ru(bpy)3 2+derivative with NCNDs as coreactant was developed in ECL systems. Fig. 3B demonstrates the ECL intensity in the presence of NCNDs as co-reactant when covalently linked to Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) in comparison to uncoupled components. As illustrated for NCNDs-Ru (curve b), the ECL signal response 120% increase compared to uncoupled NCNDs and Ru-complex (curve a). So, the intermolecular electron transfers between Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)+2 and the NCNDs in NCNDs- Ru-complex decreased the electron transfer distance compared to the intramolecular condition when Ru-complex and NCNDs components are individual. Furthermore, MOFs can be served as new nanomaterial to immobilize abundant Ru(bpy)3 2+derivatives for improvement of ECL systems efficiency (Wang et al., 2019; Shao et al., 2018). As illustrated in Fig. 3C, the ECL emission intensity of Ru@ MOF@NCNDs (curve b) and MOF@NCNDs-Ru (curve c) are 2.07 times and 2.54 times higher respect to Ru-NCNDs (curve a). When MOFs encapsulated with the Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)+2 and decorated with Ru-NCND, the difference between ECL intensity for Ru@MOF@NCND-Ru and Ru-NCND was about 370 a.u. (Fig. 3D), which increased 1.71 folds compared to MOF@NCND-Ru platform. This result indicated the covalently linking of NCND with Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) complex and also decoration of Ru-NCND complexes on Ru@MOF effectively improve the analytical performance of the proposed ECL immunosensor due to the shorter electron transfer distance and loading large amount of luminophore. Finally, when the SmS2 QD loaded onto Ru@MOF@NCNC–Ru, the ECL signal of the immunosensor due to facilitate the generating of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) 2+ radicals was enhanced (curve c). So, synergistic effects of NCNDs covalently attached onto Ru-complex and also SmS2 QDs loaded onto MOF improved the ECL response of the probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ECL efficiency of Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2)@MOF@NCND- Ru(bpy)2(phen-NH2) platform
ce:section.ce:para:
ECL quantum efficiency is distinguished as the ratio of the number of emitted photons to electrons transferred during the chemiluminescent procedure. The ECL efficiency of Ru@MOF@NCND- Ru was calculated using the [Ru(bpy)3]2+/TPrA system as the standard through the following relation(Cui et al., 2020), at scan rate of 100 mV s−1:
Q =
∅ x
∅ 0
=
[
∫
0 t
I d t
∫
0 t
i d t
]
x
/
[
∫
0 t
I d t
∫
0 t
i d t
]
0

Here, Øx represents the ECL efficiency of Ru@MOF@NCND-Ru, Ø0 represents the ECL efficiency of [Ru(bpy)3]2+/TPrA, I stand for ECL intensity, and i refer to the current value. The relative ECL efficiency is calculated to be 13.34% which comparable or better than some ECL platforms (Table S2). Also, the ECL efficiency of Ru@MOF, MOF@NCNDs-Ru and NCNDs-NCNDs-Ru were 8.46, 10.17 and 2.65% respectively. Obviously, the ECL efficiency of Ru@MOF@NCND-Ru is relatively high, due to increase the loading amount of luminophores in MOF network and also increasing of electron transfer kinetic for interamolecular reaction of NCND-Ru.

In order to obtain the high performances of the fabricated ECL immunosensor, the experimental parameters including the incubation time of CA 19-9 antigen with related antibody, the concentration of NCNDs and SmS2 QD were optimized and details presented in SI.

To characterize the stepwise modiﬁcation process of the immunosensor, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is an effective approach. The ITO electrode modified with nafion/graphdiyne has a small semicircle, indicating that graphdiyne had good conductivity (Fig. 4 A, curve a). After that Ru@MOF@NCND-Ru@SmS2 QD decorated on the surface of the modified electrode, the electron-transfer resistance (Ret) increased because the formed Ru@MOF@NCND-Ru@SmS2 QD largely prevented the electron transport (curve b). When Ab1 bonded on the MOF enriched ruthenium (II), the electron transfer resistance increased, owing to the fact that Ab1 obstructed the electron transfer (curve c). Then, increases of the Ret were attained by incubation of BSA, which can be ascribed to blocking property of BSA (curve d). Finally, incubating of the modified electrode with CA19-9 antigen, leading to increase the value of Ret because the CA19-9 antigen hindered the electron transfer of redox probe (curve e). In order to further prove that the proposed immunosensor was fabricated successfully, the stepwise fabrication process of the immunosensor was also characterized by ECL (Fig. S8). Compared with the bare electrode (curve a), the ECL response was enhanced greatly after Ru@MOF@NCNDs-Ru@SmS2 QDs/graphdiyne was immobilized on the surface of ITO electrode (curve b). Subsequently, a gradual decrease in the ECL signal was achieved when CA 19-9 antibody (curve c), BSA (curve d) and CA 19-9 antigens (curve e) was modified onto the electrode surface successively, indicating the electron transfer was hindered by the isolated biomacromolecules. Both impedance and ECL results clearly demonstrated the prosperous fabrication of the proposed ECL immunosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity, stability and reproducibility of the ECL immunosensor
ce:section.ce:para: As indicated in Fig. 4B, negligible change of ECL signal for some tumor markers such as CA15-3, CA125, AFP, CEA and also HBsAg was obtained, while the ECL change response for CA19-9 antigens is flagrant relative to that of other antigens, that revealing the superior selectivity of the ECL immunosensor for CA19-9 measurement. Additionally, the interference of co-existing compounds in real samples such as glucose, uric acid and cholesterol on the immunosensor response was also investigated. As indicated in Fig. S9 interference effect of the proposed biololcules in CA 19-9 antigen detection in negligible, so, the introduced immunoassay is highly selective towards the detection of CA 19-9 in serum samples. The reproducibility of the constructed immunosensor was checked by measuring the ECL intensity of CA19-9 antigen (0.15 UmL−1) using five parallel tests. The calculated RSD value was 4.31% that indicated an acceptable reproducibility of the ECL immunosensor. To appraise the precision of the ECL assay, the ECL intensities of the proposed immunosensor was measured during 10 consequence potential scan. As shown in Fig. 4C, the ECL intensities exhibit no distinct change and the calculated relative standard deviation (RSD) was 2.02%, that showing the outstanding stability of the present immunosensor towards CA19-9 antigen analysis. In addition, when the immunosensor stored in 4 °C for 3 weeks, the current response declines to approximately 97% of its initial response. This result indicated the good stability of the fabricated immunosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the ECL immunosensor in CA19-9 detection
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal experimental conditions, the proposed imunosensor was used for quantitative measuring of antigen at different concentration. As demonstrated in Fig. 4D, the ECL intensities gradually decreased with increasing concentration of CA19-9 from 0.0005 U mL−1 to 200 U mL−1. The calibration curve exhibited a good linear relationship between ECL signals and the logarithm of the CA19-9 antigen concentrations (inset Fig. 4D). The corresponding linear regression equation of ECL signals was ΔI = 110.5lg[c/(U/mL)] +499.01(R2 = 0.9908) (where ΔI is the difference between the ECL intensity in the absence and presence of analyte and c is the concentration of CA19-9) and the calculated detection limit was 0.00013 U mL−1 (S/N = 3). The obtained analytical characteristics of the presented immnosensor are comparable or better than presented values for other CA19-9 biosensors (Table S3). It was found that the fabricated ECL immunosensor has wider linear detection range and relatively lower detection limit, which demonstrated this proposed assay, has the excellent sensitivity for accurate measurement of CA19-9 tumor marker.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical application of the immunosensor in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To validate the applicability and feasibility of this ECL immunoassay, the recovery testing in the human serum containing CA19-9 antigen was examined and the obtained results were compared with ELISA as the standard method (Table 1 ). The results obviously indicated a good agreement between obtained results from introduced method and ELISA as a standard method.Furthermore, the accuracy of the obtained results was evaluated by human serum samples spiked with standard values of CA19-9 antigen. In this work, human serum samples were obtained from the Nour Clinical laboratory in Sanandaj of Iran. Since the linear range of the immunosensor was from 0.0005 U mL−1 to 200 U mL−1, so the two serum samples were diluted with phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.4). As shown in Table 1, 0.5 U mL−1, 10 U mL−1 and 50 U mL−1 of CA19-9 were added to the human serum specimens. The recoveries were in the range of 97.85%–101.55% and 98.12%–101.3% with the RSD of 2.45%–3.62%. The satisfactory results illustrated that our proposed ECL immunosensor has acceptable accuracy for the determination of CA19-9 tumor marker in serum samples in clinical diagnostics.

In this study, an integrated system containing MOF enriched Ru-complexes, and Ru-complexes covalently attached to NCNDs and SmS2 QD as luminophores and coreactants, and GDY as electrochemical platform which impressively accelerated the electron transfer was used as novel label-free self-enhanced ECL system. The proposed triple signal amplification strategy was introduced for the fabrication of immunosensor and its application in tumor marker detection. The NCND, which covalently linked to Ru-complex as effective coreactant, has been improved the ECL efficiency due to increasing the intramolecular electron transfer and decreasing of emitter-coreactant electron transfer path. Furthermore, decoration of SmS2 QD as a supporting coreactor onto Ru@MOF@ NCNDs-Ru was applied for improving of ECL efficiency. The ECL signal-amplified strategy was improved in the presence of graphdiyne (GDY) nanosheets as a novel and efficient platform for accelerating the electron transfer due to decreasing the band gap of Ru-complex, and this result has been proven by DFT calculations. The proposed ECL platform was used for immobilization of anti-CA19-9 antibody and fabrication of stable, sensitive and selective immunosensor for CA19-9 tumor marker analysis at the broad linear range with low detection limit. In view of these advantages, the integrated ECL platform as an attractive candidate provided a new perspective for the development of high-performance ECL based assays and their applications in fabrication of laboratory biodevices, biological analysis and clinical diagnosis.

Delnia Bahari: Doing the experiment, Writing – review & editing. Bahareh Babamiri: Synthesis, characterization, Formal analysis. Kayvan Moradi: Density Functional Theory (DFT) calculation. Abdollah Salimi: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Rahman Hallaj: Design of ECL cell system.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Iranian Nanotechnology Initiative and the Research Office of the University of Kurdistan (grant number 4.1404086). We thanks Noor Clinical Laboratory to accommodate serum samples and ELISA analysis.
